title: NOC 75210 – Boat and cable ferry operators and related occupations | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 75210 professionals are the essential maritime connectors of Canada’s coastal and inland waterways. As a TEER 3 occupation (reclassified from Skill Level C), these operators manage small vessels, cable ferries, and tour boats that transport passengers and freight across rivers, lakes, and coastal inlets. In 2026, as Canada invests in Electric Ferry Fleets and "Blue Economy" tourism, these operators are classified as Essential Marine Infrastructure Talent. For immigration, this role is a strong candidate for the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP) and the BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS) stream.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Level3Major Group75 – Helpers and labourers and other transport drivers, operators and labourersSub-Major Group752 – Other transport drivers, operators and related occupationsMinor Group7521 – Boat and cable ferry operators and related occupationsNOC Code75210

duties: Vessel Operation: Operate small boats or cable ferries to transport passengers, vehicles, and freight across short distances.
Navigation: Follow set courses and navigate vessels safely, taking into account weather conditions, water currents, and other marine traffic.
Safety & Security: Ensure the safety of passengers during boarding and transit; demonstrate the use of life jackets and emergency equipment.
Maintenance: Perform routine maintenance on engines, winches, and cables; ensure the vessel is clean and fueled/charged.
Logistics: Direct the loading and unloading of vehicles and passengers to maintain vessel balance and stability.
Communication: Maintain radio contact with shore stations and other vessels; monitor weather reports and water level alerts.
Record Keeping: Maintain daily logs of trips, passenger counts, and any incidents or maintenance performed.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is typically required.

Certifications (Mandatory):A Small Vessel Operator Proficiency (SVOP) certificate is required for most small commercial vessels.MED (Marine Emergency Duties) certification is a standard safety requirement.A valid Radio Operator's Certificate (ROC-M) is required for operating marine radios.
A Small Vessel Operator Proficiency (SVOP) certificate is required for most small commercial vessels.
MED (Marine Emergency Duties) certification is a standard safety requirement.
A valid Radio Operator's Certificate (ROC-M) is required for operating marine radios.
Training: Several months of on-the-job training are usually provided, especially for cable ferry operations.
Health & Safety: A marine medical certificate and a basic first aid certificate are often required.
Physical fitness for outdoor work and excellent vision (including color vision for buoy identification) are essential.
salary_prospects: Note: In 2026, experienced operators in major municipal transit systems (e.g., Vancouver's False Creek Ferries or Toronto Island Ferries) often earn annual salaries between $58,000 and $75,000 inclusive of benefits and overtime.
job_outlook: The outlook is Stable. While automated docking technologies are being explored, the "Human-in-the-Loop" requirement for passenger safety, emergency response, and navigating unpredictable water conditions ensures that professional operators will remain the standard through 2033. Projections indicate a steady increase in demand as coastal cities look toward "Blue Highways" to relieve road congestion.
labour_market_demand: The demand for boat and ferry operators in 2026 is Good, particularly in provinces with extensive water networks. In British Columbia, Ontario (Great Lakes), and the Atlantic provinces, a combination of expanding tourism and the need for reliable transport to island communities has created a steady hiring environment. According to 2026 maritime industry reports, there is a specific shortage of operators with Small Vessel Machinery Operator (SVMO) certification to manage the new generation of hybrid-electric transit vessels.
Maritime Innovation Frontier: Gain experience operating eco-friendly electric cable ferries and modern passenger vessels in some of the world's most scenic waterways.
Essential Public Service: Many ferry operator roles are government-funded or municipal positions, offering excellent job security and public sector benefits.
Diverse Career Path: Start by operating small passenger vessels and progress toward Master or Mate certifications for larger commercial ships.
Strategic PR Pathways: This TEER 3 role is a recognized category for Provincial Nominee Programs (PNP) in coastal regions, offering a streamlined route to Permanent Residency.
pathways: Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A high-conversion route for operators with a job offer from a designated employer in Newfoundland, NS, NB, or PEI.
BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): A direct route for workers currently employed in British Columbia’s maritime transport sector.
New 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A dedicated stream for 33,000 workers already in Canada, specifically targeting essential transport and tourism-related roles.
Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RNIP): Ideal for operators willing to work in water-dependent communities in Ontario or the Prairies.
